note_data.title: Homeostasis
keyPoints: Cannot extract homeostasis content - only table of contents visible in source

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give a sequence of events or detailed account of structure
State similarities AND differences between two things

modelAnswer.question: Explain how blood glucose concentration is controlled by negative feedback when it rises above normal. [6]
modelAnswer.answer: When blood glucose concentration rises above normal, this is detected by beta cells in the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas. The beta cells secrete insulin into the blood. Insulin binds to receptors on liver and muscle cells, activating enzymes that convert glucose to glycogen. This process is called glycogenesis. Glucose is also taken up by cells for respiration. As a result, blood glucose concentration decreases back to normal, completing the negative feedback loop.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Detection by beta cells in islets of Langerhans
Mark 3: Insulin binds to receptors on target cells
Mark 4: Conversion of glucose to glycogen/glycogenesis
Mark 6: Blood glucose returns to normal

note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Nephron structure showing glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, distal convoluted tubule, and collecting duct with associated blood vessels
pastPaperRefs: M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain how the body maintains constant blood glucose levels, including the roles of insulin and glucagon
O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing knowledge of which hormone is released when blood glucose is too high
M/J 2022 P2 Q6(b) (4 marks) — they required students to describe the negative feedback mechanism in temperature regulation
O/N 2023 P2 Q3(c) (5 marks) — students had to explain why homeostasis is important for enzyme function and give specific examples

workedExample.question: Explain how the human body responds when blood glucose concentration becomes too high. (5 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the stimulus - blood glucose concentration rises above normal levels
Step 2: State the receptor - specialized cells in the pancreas (islets of Langerhans) detect the change
Step 3: Identify the response mechanism - pancreas releases the hormone insulin into the bloodstream
Step 4: Describe insulin's effects - insulin causes liver and muscle cells to take up glucose and convert it to glycogen for storage
Step 5: Explain the negative feedback - as blood glucose falls back to normal, insulin release decreases, maintaining homeostasis
workedExample.answer: When blood glucose becomes too high, specialized cells in the pancreas detect this change. The pancreas responds by releasing insulin into the bloodstream. Insulin causes liver and muscle cells to absorb excess glucose and convert it into glycogen for storage. This lowers blood glucose back to normal levels. As glucose returns to normal, insulin release decreases, completing the negative feedback loop.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for detecting high glucose, 1 mark for pancreas/insulin, 1 mark for glucose uptake by cells, 1 mark for conversion to glycogen, 1 mark for negative feedback concept

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused positive and negative feedback' — always specify that homeostatic mechanisms use NEGATIVE feedback to reverse changes
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often forgot to mention the receptor in feedback loops' — always include: stimulus → receptor → response → effect in your answers
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates frequently mixed up insulin and glucagon effects' — remember insulin LOWERS blood glucose, glucagon RAISES it
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a negative feedback loop diagram showing: 1) Normal level baseline, 2) Change detected (stimulus), 3) Receptor (e.g., pancreas), 4) Response (hormone release), 5) Effect (corrective action), 6) Return to normal with feedback arrow. Include specific examples like blood glucose control with insulin/glucagon or temperature control with sweating/shivering.
